author: @AtlasReburdened
text: I still think the design is a bit inferior to a standard vertical wind turbine for almost all situations. Given that the averaged vector of the wind is restricted to the horizontal plane by the existence of the ground, it seems unnecessary to be able to harness wind from every direction(save for at the top corners of very tall buildings), and the very architecture of the device which permits it to harness wind from any direction necessarily decreases it's efficiency in harnessing wind with a horizontal vector. It's a delightful curiosity, but I think I'd go with a vertical turbine in any case of practicality.

author: @Yaman-D-Chhaya
text: Rob Sir, Cuboctahedron should have a total of 8 faces as the name implies octa on your device I see 6 faces so can it be called a cubhexahedron since it has 6 faces? Just a query Rob Sir!! Love and regards from India.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: If you'll note, it has 8 triangular faces, along with the 6 square faces.  The "cub" (i.e. "cube") in the name is referencing the 6 square faces and the "octa" represents the 8 triangular faces.  In the case of Rob's wind turbine, the triangular faces are all open, so it's easily missed.

author: @JSabh
text: That looks and is awesome. Cheers BTW, saw the coffee cup and had to laugh to myself as I thought " Coffee with Rob" with my cup in hand lol. I have been archiving your videos and saving them offline as a kind of reference data base along with some other content that is related. If you sold the offline version of a library using your videos already produced, you may profit well and you would be doing something no other youtube content provider is (that I know of). Organize them onto a flash drive and sell it as you would a book. After all, these phones are really highly efficient computers that can store the encyclopedia Britannica ten times over but, if your reference library is dependent upon Youtube and a network being available, then youtube could lose or delete all of your hard work or the network could fail and it would be lost still. That is my reasoning for saving your content on multiple drives. Something to think about.

replies.author: @wizrom3046
replies.text:
@Ed.R I'm agreeing with you Ed R, and I have raised this point a few times on this channel.
All he needs to do is add a resistor and show the voltage and current measurements (ie; power produced) and the experiment becomes useful, instead of just a novelty.
It is incredibly low effort BUT will raise the value of the video by a great amount.
It is a shame, this could be such a good channel if he simply bought a second multimeter and a few resistors... Especially after spending many hours constructing the experiment all he needs to do is add a resistor and ammeter which takes 30 seconds. Sigh.

author: @rhoniandjeff7453
text: That's really awesome sir! I like so much I want one lol. Very awesome finished look. That is really nice.

replies.author: @petermiddleton6902
replies.text: @Ed.R Your quite right in some regards, although no one makes 50V @ 3000F super caps unfortunately at present, and they woud be exactly the same size as your 2.7V @ 3000F super cap. High voltage super caps are completely technically possible, and the energy storage would be phenomenal!. Eg 1041.7 Watt hrs!, dozens of times the energy density than the worlds best lithium battery technology. Thats why HV super cap tech is seriously worth researching and developing. Would be a total game changer indeed!...;)

replies.author: @petermiddleton6902
replies.text: @Ed.R Yes the 1000mAh 16340 lithium ion cell has a much greater energy capacity than the present 2.7V 3000F super capacitor technology. But if a HV 50V+ 3000F super capacitor was developed, it would have a substantially higher energy capacity than any presently known lithium ion battery chemistry. By their inherent nature battery chemistries always will have a limited voltage window. Where as super capacitors are mainly limited by the breakdown voltage of their electrolytes. Special HV capacitor  electrolytes have reached even upto 400V in the lab. With the rest of the development is in perfecting the electrode materials, construction and reducing current leakage. Then once solved, which is mainly an engineering challenge, super capacitor technology will be like a quantum leap and revolution in ultra high energy density & affordable energy storage. Far beyond than any presently or technically conceivable battery chemistry, including the latest in lithium air to even strontium fluoride battery concepts. Hope that all helps :)

replies.author: @petermiddleton6902
replies.text: For example: A 400V @ 3000F HV next generation super capacitor the same size of a 2.7V @ 3000F present tech super cap, would have an mind boggling energy storage potential of 6.6 Megawatt hrs!!. Thats why this next generation HV super cap technology would be so truly revolutionary once perfected in due course. And even if we get remotely close to that as well, eg a HV super cap storing kWhs that can fit in the palm of ones hand, certainly very much within reach within the next couple of years of development.

replies.author: @petermiddleton6902
replies.text: @Ed.R Each one of those 2.7V 3000F super caps store around 3Whrs. The problem is when you connect 19 of them in series then you will also loose 19 times their farad capacity. This is basic electrical engineering 101 when ever you connect capacitors in series unfortunately. With a innovative switching & isolation circuit connected to each super cap you can then keep those farads equaling 3Whrs x 19 = 57Whrs. But this does not compare at all with a single HV 50V @ 3000F next generation super cap that will store 1041Whrs. Hope that is a bit more clear on the technical issues at hand.

author: @CircuitBreaker256
text: 1000F or 1000 micro F?

replies.author: @petermiddleton6902
replies.text: 1000 microfarads :)

replies.author: @bernardstokes6550
replies.text: @petermiddleton6902 has to be at that voltage,

replies.author: @petermiddleton6902
replies.text: @bernardstokes6550 50V rated electrolytic caps are quite standard in that uF range right up to 5000uF at very low cost (the higher the uF the better for smoothing buffers i find). Really depends on what your generator output voltage turns out to be at the high end. But if your using a stepper motor or more powerful RC brushless motor as a gen (with a 3phase bridge rectifier or homebrew diode array), then standard 50V rated caps acting as smoothing buffer should be more than enough. Hope that all helps :)

replies.author: @petermiddleton6902
replies.text: Ps. You can get some awesome 10,000uf 50V caps, but they also tend to be alot more pricey for the uf capacity. So if required you could always connect the cheaper 5000uf caps in parallel arrays, to build up your uf to whatever works the best for your output on higher output gen amperage. Then feed the juice into a cheap MPPT solar charge controller for deep cycle lead acid or zinc/ bromide/ iodine battery storage. Or use a suitable regulated lithium charge controller for 18650 battery packs for your micro offgrid energy system etc.

replies.author: @bernardstokes6550
